subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of the quadratic equation $x^{2} \sin \theta-x(\sin \theta \cos \theta+1)+\cos \theta=0\left(0  < \theta < 45^{\circ}\right),$ and

$\alpha < \beta .$ Then $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\alpha^{n}+\frac{(-1)^{n}}{\beta^{n}}\right)$ is equal to :
question:
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of the quadratic equation $x^{2} \sin \theta-x(\sin \theta \cos \theta+1)+\cos \theta=0\left(0  < \theta < 45^{\circ}\right),$ and

$\alpha < \beta .$ Then $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\alpha^{n}+\frac{(-1)^{n}}{\beta^{n}}\right)$ is equal to :
solution:
$x^{2} \sin \theta-x(\sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta+1)+\cos \theta=0$.

$x^{2} \sin \theta-x \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta-x+\cos \theta=0$

$x \sin \theta(x-\cos \theta)-1(x-\cos \theta)=0$

$(x-\cos \theta)(x \sin \theta-1)=0$

$\therefore x=\cos \theta, \operatorname{cosec} \theta, \theta \in\left(0,45^{\circ}\right)$

$\therefore \alpha=\cos \theta, \beta=\operatorname{cosec} \theta$

$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \alpha^{n}=1+\cos \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta+\ldots \infty=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta}$

$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n}}{\beta^{n}}=1-\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec} \theta}+\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta}-\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^{3} \theta}+\ldots \infty$

$=1-\sin \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta-\sin ^{3} \theta+\ldots \infty$

$=\frac{1}{1+\sin \theta}$

$\therefore \quad \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\alpha^{n}+\frac{(-1)^{n}}{\beta^{n}}\right)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \alpha^{n}+\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n}}{\beta^{n}}$

$=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta}+\frac{1}{1+\sin \theta} .$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \(\alpha, \beta \in\) be roots of equation \(x^2-70 x+\lambda=0\), where \(\frac{\lambda}{2}, \frac{\lambda}{3} \notin\). If \(\lambda\) assumes the minimum possible value, then \(\frac{(\sqrt{\alpha-1}+\sqrt{\beta-1})(\lambda+35)}{\boldsymbol{|\alpha-\beta|}}\) is equal to :
question: Let \(\alpha, \beta \in\) be roots of equation \(x^2-70 x+\lambda=0\), where \(\frac{\lambda}{2}, \frac{\lambda}{3} \notin\). If \(\lambda\) assumes the minimum possible value, then \(\frac{(\sqrt{\alpha-1}+\sqrt{\beta-1})(\lambda+35)}{\boldsymbol{|\alpha-\beta|}}\) is equal to :
solution:
Given,

x2-70x+λ=0

Sum of the roots will be α+β=70

Product of roots will be αβ=λ

⇒α(70-α)=λ

⇒dλdα=70-2α

Now, λ is increasing for all α≤35

And 2 and 3 does not divide λ so taking  α=5 we get, β=70-α=65 

As for α=1, β=69 & for α=4, β=66 which is not possible as they are multiple of 2 & 3

Hence, α=5, β=65, λ=325

Now solving,

α-1+β-1λ+35|α-β|

=5-1+65-1325+35|5-65|

=2+8·36060

=60

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be a quadratic polynomial such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If one of the roots of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, then its other root lies in
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be a quadratic polynomial such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If one of the roots of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, then its other root lies in
solution:
Let fx=ax2+bx+c be a quadratic equation with roots α, β

f2+f-1=0⇒5a+b+2c=0

 Also f3=0⇒9a+3b+c=0

⇒a-5=b13=c6

Product of roots αβ=ca=-65

We know α=3⇒β=-25∈-1,0

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>β</mi></msqrt></mfrac></math>  are the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has roots:
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>β</mi></msqrt></mfrac></math>  are the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has roots:
solution:
ax2+bx+1=0 has roots 1α,1β

⇒   1α+1β=-ba      ...1

1αβ=1a    ...2

xx+b3+a3-3abx=0

⇒ x2+b3-3abx+a3=0

Multiply and divide with a3, we get

⇒   x2+a3b3a3-31abax+a3=0
From equations 1 & 2,
⇒   x2+αβ3/2-α1/2+β1/23αβ3-31αβα+βαβx+αβ3/2=0

⇒  x2-α3/2+β3/2x+α3/2β3/2=0

Roots are α32 and β32.

subject: Mathematics
q: The value of $\mathrm{k}$ for which the equation $(K-2) x^2+8 x+K+4=0$ has both roots real, distinct and negative is
question: The value of $\mathrm{k}$ for which the equation $(K-2) x^2+8 x+K+4=0$ has both roots real, distinct and negative is
solution:
$(K-2) x^2+8 x+K+4=0$
If real roots then,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 8^2-4(K-2)(K+4)>0 \\
\Rightarrow & K^2+2 K-8 < 16 \\
\Rightarrow & K^2+6 K-4 K-24 < 0 \\
\Rightarrow & (K+6)(K-4) < 0 \\
\Rightarrow & -6 < K < 4
\end{aligned}
$$
If both roots are negative then $\alpha \beta$ is $+v e$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{K+4}{K-2}>0 \Rightarrow K>-4 \\
& \text { Also, } \frac{K-2}{K+4}>0 \Rightarrow K>2
\end{aligned}
$$
Roots are real so, $-6 < K < 4$
So, 6 and 4 are not correct.
Since, $K>2$, so 1 is also not correct value of $K$.
$$
\therefore K=3
$$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> be the roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo></math> then
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> be the roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo></math> then
solution:
If α, β are roots of quadratic equation 5x2+6x-2=0

⇒ 5α2+6α-2=0 ⇒5α2+6α=2

⇒ 5β2+6β-2=0 ⇒5β2+6β=2

5S6+6S5=5α6+β6+6α5+β5

=α45α2+6α+β45β2+6β

=2α4+β4=2S4

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> are roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mfenced><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mi>β</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mi>n</mi></msup></math> for each positive integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>17</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>20</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>17</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>19</mn></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>18</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>19</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><msubsup><mi>P</mi><mn>18</mn><mn>2</mn></msubsup></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> are roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mfenced><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mi>β</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mi>n</mi></msup></math> for each positive integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>17</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>20</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>17</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>19</mn></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>18</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>19</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><msubsup><mi>P</mi><mn>18</mn><mn>2</mn></msubsup></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given, x2+52x+10=0

and  Pn=αn-βn 

Now P17P20+52P17P19P18P19+52P182=P17P20+52P19P18P19+52P18

=P17α20-β20+52α19-β19P18α19-β19+52α18-β18

=P17α19α+52-β19β+52P18α18α+52-β18β+52

Since α+52=-10/α       ...1

& β+52=-10/β       ...2

Now put these values in above expression

P17α19α+52-β19β+52P18α18α+52-β18β+52=-10P17P18-10P18P17=1

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>S</mi></math>, be the set of all real roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
question: Let <math><mi>S</mi></math>, be the set of all real roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
solution:
Let 3x=t
tt-1+2=t-1+t-2
t2-t+2=t-1+t-2

Let, ft=t2-t+2 & gt=t-1+t-2

ft & gt has a solution at t=a.
3x=a ⇒x=log3⁡a.
So, it is a singleton set.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> be the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> be the consecutive terms of a non-constant G.P and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>β</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> be the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> be the consecutive terms of a non-constant G.P and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>β</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is:
solution:
Given: α and β be the roots of equation px2+qx-r=0

So, α+β=-qp, αβ=-rp

It is given that, 1α+1β=34

⇒α+βαβ=34

⇒qr=34

Now, p,q,r are in GP

So, common ratio of this GP will be qp=rq=43

⇒px2+qx-r=0

⇒x2+qpx-rp=0

⇒x2+43x-432=0

⇒9x2+12x-16=0

⇒α-β2=α+β2-4αβ

⇒α-β2=-1292-4-169

⇒α-β2=169+649

⇒α-β2=809

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>32</mn><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>32</mn><mrow><msup><mi>sec</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>81</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is :
question: The number of solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>32</mn><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>32</mn><mrow><msup><mi>sec</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>81</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
Given, 32tan2x+32sec2x=81,     0≤x≤π4

32tan2x+321+tan2x=81

32tan2x+32.32tan2x=81

33×32tan2x=81

32tan2x=2711

tan2x=ln322711
tanx=ln322711∈0,1
One solution in 0,π4

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> are the roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2023</mn><msup><mi>α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2024</mn><msup><mi>β</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msup></math>, then
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> are the roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2023</mn><msup><mi>α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2024</mn><msup><mi>β</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msup></math>, then
solution:
Given: α, β are roots of x2-x-1=0.

⇒α2-α-1=0

⇒α2=α+1   ...i

Also, Sn=2023αn+2024βn

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=2023αn-1+2024βn-1+2023αn-2+2024βn-2

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=2023αn-21+α+2024βn-21+β

Using equation i,

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=2023αn-2α2+2024βn-2β2

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=2023αn+2024βn

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=Sn

Putting, n=12

⇒S12=S11+S10

q: If <math><mi>x</mi></math> is a solution of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> , then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt></math>  is equal to :
question: If <math><mi>x</mi></math> is a solution of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> , then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt></math>  is equal to :
solution:
2x+1- 2x-1=1    ...1

⇒2x+1+2x-1-24x2-1=1

 ⇒4x-1=24x2-1

 ⇒16x2-8x+1=16x2-4

⇒8x=5

 ⇒x=58 which satisfies equation 1.

So, 4x2-1=34

subject: Mathematics
q: If one real root of the quadratic equation $81 x^{2}+k x+256=0$ is cube of the other root, then a value of $\mathrm{k}$ is :
question: If one real root of the quadratic equation $81 x^{2}+k x+256=0$ is cube of the other root, then a value of $\mathrm{k}$ is :
solution:
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of the equation, $81 x^{2}+k x+256=0$

Given $(\alpha)^{\frac{1}{3}}=\beta \Rightarrow \alpha=\beta^{3}$

$\because \quad$ Product of the roots $=\frac{256}{81}$

$\therefore \quad(\alpha)(\beta)=\frac{256}{81}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \beta^{4}=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{4} \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{64}{27}$

$\because$ Sum of the roots $=-\frac{k}{81}$

$\therefore \quad \alpha+\beta=-\frac{k}{81} \Rightarrow \frac{4}{3}+\frac{64}{27}=-\frac{k}{81}$

$\Rightarrow \quad k=-300$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $p$ and $q$ are non-zero real numbers and $\alpha^3+\beta^3=-p, \alpha \beta=q$, then a quadratic equation whose roots are $\frac{\alpha^2}{\beta}, \frac{\beta^2}{\alpha}$ is :
question: If $p$ and $q$ are non-zero real numbers and $\alpha^3+\beta^3=-p, \alpha \beta=q$, then a quadratic equation whose roots are $\frac{\alpha^2}{\beta}, \frac{\beta^2}{\alpha}$ is :
solution:
Given $\alpha^3+\beta^3=-p$ and $\alpha \beta=q$
Let $\frac{\alpha^2}{\beta}$ and $\frac{\beta^2}{\alpha}$ be the root of required quadratic equation.
So, $\frac{\alpha^2}{\beta}+\frac{\beta^2}{\alpha}=\frac{\alpha^3+\beta^3}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{-p}{q}$
and $\frac{\alpha^2}{\beta} \times \frac{\beta^2}{\alpha}=\alpha \beta=q$
Hence, required quadratic equation is
$$
\begin{aligned}
&x^2-\left(\frac{-p}{q}\right) x+q=0 \\
&\Rightarrow x^2+\frac{p}{q} x+q=0 \Rightarrow q x^2+p x+q^2=0
\end{aligned}
$$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math> are the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>q</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>β</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>β</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to :
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math> are the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>q</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>β</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>β</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to :

α, β are roots of x2+px+2=0

1α,1β are roots of  2x2+2qx+1=0 

∴ α, β are roots of x2+2qx+2=0

 ⇒α+β=-2q, αβ=2

∴p=2q  ...(i)

⇒α-1αβ-1βα+1ββ+1α=αβ-αβ-βα+1αβ·αβ+1αβ+2

⇒2-α+β2-2αβαβ+12·2+12+2

⇒52-p2-42·92=949-p2 or, 949-4q2

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2+2 \sqrt{2} x-1=0$. The quadratic equation, whose roots are $\alpha^4+\beta^4$ and $\frac{1}{10}\left(\alpha^6+\beta^6\right)$, is :
question: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2+2 \sqrt{2} x-1=0$. The quadratic equation, whose roots are $\alpha^4+\beta^4$ and $\frac{1}{10}\left(\alpha^6+\beta^6\right)$, is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & x^2+2 \sqrt{2} x-1=0 \\ & \alpha+\beta=-2 \sqrt{2} \\ & \alpha \beta=-1 \\ & \alpha^4+\beta^4=\left(\alpha^2+\beta^2\right)^2-2 \alpha^2 \beta^2 \\ & =\left((\alpha+\beta)^2-2 \alpha \beta\right)^2-2(\alpha \beta)^2 \\ & =(8+2)^2-2(-1)^2 \\ & =100-2=98 \\ & \alpha^6+\beta^6=\left(\alpha^3+\beta^3\right)^2-2 \alpha^3 \beta^3 \\ & =\left((\alpha+\beta)\left((\alpha+\beta)^2-3 \alpha \beta\right)^2-2(\alpha \beta)^3\right.\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =(-2 \sqrt{2}(8+3))^2+2 \\ & =(8)(121)+2=970 \\ & \frac{1}{10}\left(\alpha^6+\beta^6\right)=97 \\ & x^2-(98+97) x+(98)(97)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-195 x+9506=0\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are roots of the equation, $x^2-4 \sqrt{2} k x+2 e^{4 \ln k}-1=0$ for some $k$, and $\alpha^2+\beta^2=66$, then $\alpha^3+\beta^3$ is equal to:
question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are roots of the equation, $x^2-4 \sqrt{2} k x+2 e^{4 \ln k}-1=0$ for some $k$, and $\alpha^2+\beta^2=66$, then $\alpha^3+\beta^3$ is equal to:
solution:
$x^2-4 \sqrt{2} k x+2 e^{4 \ln k}-1=0$ or, $x^2-4 \sqrt{2} k x+2 k^4-1=0$ $\alpha+\beta=4 \sqrt{2} k$ and $\alpha \cdot \beta=2 k^4-1$ Squaring both sides, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\alpha+\beta)^2=(4 \sqrt{2} k)^2 \\
&\Rightarrow \alpha^2+\beta^2+2 \alpha \beta=32 k^2 \\
&66+2 \alpha \beta=32 k^2 \\
&66+2\left(2 k^4-1\right)=32 k^2 \\
&66+4 k^4-2=32 k^2 \\
&\Rightarrow 4 k^4-32 k^2+64=0 \\
&\text { or, } k^4-8 k^2+16=0 \\
&\Rightarrow\left(k^2\right)^2-8 k^2+16=0 \\
&\Rightarrow\left(k^2-4\right)\left(k^2-4\right)=0 \\
&\Rightarrow k^2=4, k^2=4 \\
&\Rightarrow k=\pm 2
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $\alpha^3+\beta^3=(\alpha+\beta)\left(\alpha^2+\beta^2-\alpha \beta\right)$
$$
\therefore \alpha^3+\beta^3=(4 \sqrt{2} k)\left[66-\left(2 k^4-1\right)\right]
$$
Putting $k=-2,(k=+2$ cannot be taken because it does not satisfy the above equation)
$\therefore \alpha^3+\beta^3=(4 \sqrt{2}(-2))\left[66-2(-2)^4-1\right]$
$\alpha^3+\beta^3=(-8 \sqrt{2})(66-32+1)=$
$(-8 \sqrt{2})(35)$
$\therefore \alpha^3+\beta^3=-280 \sqrt{2}$

question: If $\lambda \in \mathrm{R}$ is such that the sum of the cubes of the roots of the equation, $x^2+(2-\lambda) x+(10-\lambda)=0$ is minimum, then the magnitude of the difference of the roots of this equation is

$$

$$
The magnitude of the difference of the roots is $\left|\sqrt{\lambda^2-36}\right|$

So, $\alpha^3+\beta^3=\frac{(\lambda-2)^3}{4}+\frac{3(\lambda-2)\left(\lambda^2-36\right)}{4}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
=\frac{(\lambda-2)\left(4 \lambda^2-4 \lambda-104\right)}{4} \\
=&(\lambda-2)\left(\lambda^2-\lambda-26\right)=f(\lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$
As $f(\lambda)$ attains its minimum value at $\lambda=4$.
Therefore, the magintude of the difference of the

q: The number of real solutions of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is:
question: The number of real solutions of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is:
solution:
We have, x2-x-12=0

⇒x2-4x+3x-12=0

⇒x+3x-4=0

⇒x=4 as x≠-3

⇒x=±2

Hence, number of real solution is 2.

q: The sum, of the squares of all the roots of the equation $x^2+|2 x-3|-4=0$, is
question: The sum, of the squares of all the roots of the equation $x^2+|2 x-3|-4=0$, is
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { For } x \geq \frac{3}{2} \\ & x^2+2 x-3-4=0 \\ & x^2+2 x-7=0 \\ & x=\frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4+28}}{2}=-1 \pm 2 \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$
Only $2 \sqrt{2}-1$ is acceptable root
For $x < \frac{3}{2}$
$x^2-2 x+3-4=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& x^2-2 x-1=0 \\
& x=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4+4}}{2}=1 \pm \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}$
Only $1-\sqrt{2}$ is acceptable
Sum of the square $=(1-\sqrt{2})^2+(2 \sqrt{2}-1)^2$
$=6(2-\sqrt{2})$

Then the equation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mfenced></math> denote the greatest integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>≤</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>. Let x=t

t2+2t−3=0

t=1, −3

x∈−3, −2∪1, 2

q: The minimum value of the sum of the squares of the roots of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is
question: The minimum value of the sum of the squares of the roots of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is
solution:
Let α,β be the roots of the equation

x2+3-ax+1-2a=0

Then, sum of roots α+β=a-3

And product of roots αβ=1-2a

We know that α2+β2=α+β2-2αβ

∴α2+β2=a-32-21-2a

=a2-2a+7

=a-12+6

∴ Minimum value of α2+β2=6 at a=1.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let one root of the quadratic equation in $x$: $(k^2 - 15k + 27)x^2 + 9(k-1)x + 18 = 0$ be twice the other. Then the length of the latus rectum of the parabola $y^2 = 6kx$ is equal to:
question: Let one root of the quadratic equation in $x$: $(k^2 - 15k + 27)x^2 + 9(k-1)x + 18 = 0$ be twice the other. Then the length of the latus rectum of the parabola $y^2 = 6kx$ is equal to:
solution:
Let the roots of the given quadratic equation be $\alpha$ and $2\alpha$.

Sum of the roots:
$\alpha + 2\alpha = \dfrac{-9(k-1)}{k^2 - 15k + 27}$
$3\alpha = \dfrac{-9(k-1)}{k^2 - 15k + 27} \Rightarrow \alpha = \dfrac{-3(k-1)}{k^2 - 15k + 27}$

Product of the roots:
$\alpha \cdot 2\alpha = \dfrac{18}{k^2 - 15k + 27}$
$2\alpha^2 = \dfrac{18}{k^2 - 15k + 27} \Rightarrow \alpha^2 = \dfrac{9}{k^2 - 15k + 27}$

Substituting the value of $\alpha$ from the sum into the product equation:
$\left( \dfrac{-3(k-1)}{k^2 - 15k + 27} \right)^2 = \dfrac{9}{k^2 - 15k + 27}$

$\dfrac{9(k-1)^2}{(k^2 - 15k + 27)^2} = \dfrac{9}{k^2 - 15k + 27}$

Since $k^2 - 15k + 27 \neq 0$, we can simplify to:
$(k-1)^2 = k^2 - 15k + 27$

$k^2 - 2k + 1 = k^2 - 15k + 27$

$13k = 26 \Rightarrow k = 2$

The equation of the parabola is $y^2 = 6kx$. Substituting $k = 2$, we get:
$y^2 = 12x$

The length of the latus rectum of a parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ is $4a$, which is the coefficient of $x$.

Therefore, the length of the latus rectum is $12$.

Answer: $12$

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> are real number such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>p</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>q</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>369</mn></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>p</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>q</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> are real number such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>p</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>q</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>369</mn></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>p</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>q</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

p4+q4=369⇒p2+q22-2pq2=369

⇒p+q2-2pq2-2pq2=369

⇒9-2pq2-2pq2=369

⇒pq2-18pq-144=0  ⇒pq=24(rejected),-6

Now, 1p+1q-2=pq2p+q2=pq29=4

If <math><msub><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>k</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then which one of the following statements is not true?
question: Let <math><mi>α</mi></math> and <math><mi>β</mi></math> be the roots of the equation <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> . If <math><msub><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>k</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then which one of the following statements is not true?
solution:
α5=5α+3
β5=5β+3

P5=5α+β+6
=51+6
P5=11 and

P2=α2+β2=α+1+β+1=3
 and P3=α3+β3=2α+1+2β+1=21+2=4
P2×P3=12 and P5=11⇒P5≠P2×P3

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the distinct roots of the equation $x^2-\left(t^2-5 t+6\right) x+1=0, t \in \mathbb{R}$ and $a_n=\alpha^n+\beta^n$. Then the minimum value of $\frac{a_{2023}+a_{2025}}{a_{2024}}$ is
question: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the distinct roots of the equation $x^2-\left(t^2-5 t+6\right) x+1=0, t \in \mathbb{R}$ and $a_n=\alpha^n+\beta^n$. Then the minimum value of $\frac{a_{2023}+a_{2025}}{a_{2024}}$ is
solution:
by newton's theorem
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}+2}-\left(\mathrm{t}^2-5 \mathrm{t}+6\right) \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}+1}+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=0 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{a}_{2025}+\mathrm{a}_{2023}=\left(\mathrm{t}^2-5 \mathrm{t}+6\right) \mathrm{a}_{2024} \\
& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{a}_{2025}+\mathrm{a}_{2023}}{\mathrm{a}_{2024}}=\mathrm{t}^2-5 \mathrm{t}+6 \\
& \because \mathrm{t}^2-5 \mathrm{t}+6=\left(\mathrm{t}-\frac{5}{2}\right)^2-\frac{1}{4} \\
& \therefore \text { minimum value }=-\frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of real solutions of the equation \(x\left(x^2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2|\right)=0\) is ______.
question: The number of real solutions of the equation \(x\left(x^2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2|\right)=0\) is ______.
solution:
Given,

xx2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2|=0

⇒x=0 or x2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2|=0

We know that sum of all positive terms can never be zero,

So, x2+3|x|+5|x-1|+6|x-2| can never be zero for any value of x,

So there is no value of x which satisfies the above equation
Hence, only solution is x=0, i.e. number of solution is 1 .

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of all possible positive integral value of <math><mi>α</mi></math> for which the roots of the quadratic equation <math><mn>6</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are rational numbers is:
question: The number of all possible positive integral value of <math><mi>α</mi></math> for which the roots of the quadratic equation <math><mn>6</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are rational numbers is:
solution:
For ax2+bx+c=0, we have D=b2-4ac 

D=121-24α
For rational roots, D must be perfect square.
∴α=3, 4, 5
∴ Number of values of α=3
